He considered [revolution](/wiki/Revolution "Revolution") to be a [natural law](/wiki/Natural_law "Natural law") that reasserted itself continuously throughout history, and that as the development of capitalism had established the "necessary ground for our revolution", he foresaw that revolutionary socialists would soon "[eat the rich](/wiki/Eat_the_rich "Eat the rich")".[[218]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDrake200349-218) To Cafiero, revolution was the "sublime law of nature, law of life and of progress, law of justice and love, law of liberty and equality". He claimed that the coming social revolution would inherit the goals of all previous revolutions, and would drive the elimination of [religion](/wiki/Religion "Religion"), the [nuclear family](/wiki/Nuclear_family "Nuclear family"), [private property](/wiki/Private_property "Private property") and [authority](/wiki/Authority "Authority"). After such a revolution, he believed that a [classless society](/wiki/Classless_society "Classless society") would be formed, in which all people would live in [liberty](/wiki/Liberty "Liberty"), [equality](/wiki/Social_equality "Social equality") and [fraternity](/wiki/Fraternity_(philosophy) "Fraternity (philosophy)").[[219]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDrake200349–50-219)

While his vision of social revolution was principally concerned with the abolition of capitalism, Cafiero also concerned himself with the abolition of the [state](/wiki/State_(polity) "State (polity)"). He considered any state, even a [socialist state](/wiki/Socialist_state "Socialist state"), to present a danger to working people. He echoed Bakunin's predictions that a "[dictatorship of the proletariat](/wiki/Dictatorship_of_the_proletariat "Dictatorship of the proletariat")" would inevitably result in an authoritarian bureaucracy, which he feared would become "new and even more terrible political oppressors and economic exploiters". He proposed that in order to prevent the corrupting effects of power, a social revolution would need to establish a [stateless society](/wiki/Stateless_society "Stateless society").[[220]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDrake200350-220) Cafiero believed that government existed only to defend the privileged [elite](/wiki/Elite "Elite") and oppress the masses, a system he wished to abolish through the establishment of universal liberty and equality under anarchist socialism. Cafiero's ultimate revolutionary aim was "to take away from man the means of inflicting useless and dangerous activity on humanity."[[221]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDrake200350–51-221)

Cafiero thus argued passionately in favour of "[propaganda of the deed](/wiki/Propaganda_of_the_deed "Propaganda of the deed")",[[222]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBantman2019371,_373Drake200351-222) an idea he had developed from the earlier works of Bakunin.[[223]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBantman2019373-223) He defined propaganda of the deed as a [permanent revolution](/wiki/Permanent_revolution "Permanent revolution") carried out [by any means necessary](/wiki/By_any_means_necessary "By any means necessary"), whether it be by writing and public speaking, by violent attacks, or by [voting](/wiki/Voting "Voting").[[224]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBantman2019371Graham2019340-224) He compared it directly with the Marxist understanding of revolution, considering the two to be more or less identical in practice.[[225]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDrake200351-225) Drawing from Bakunin, Cafiero believed that any revolutionary communist had a "permanent obligation to resist capitalism" by any means necessary.[[225]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDrake200351-225) Cafiero believed that the revolutionary doctrines of Marxism and anarchism were thus opposed to [reformist socialism](/wiki/Reformism#socialism "Reformism"), which he considered to be a means of reinforcing the status quo of capitalism. To Cafiero, there could be no effective collaboration with capitalism, as capitalists deserved to be punished for their exploitation and repression of workers. He concluded that: "To diminish, reduce, or limit our program, in a parliamentary sense, is to treat with the enemy, to fold one’s battle flag, to trick the people, and to renounce the revolution."[[226]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDrake200351–52-226) Cafiero's revolutionary ideas formed the basis for the anarchist tendency of [illegalism](/wiki/Illegalism "Illegalism").[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraham2019340-173)

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carlo_Cafiero&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Legacy")]

Cafiero's legacy was left by his organisation of the Italian anarchist movement, his ideological development of [anarchist communism](/wiki/Anarchist_communism "Anarchist communism") and [insurrectionary anarchism](/wiki/Insurrectionary_anarchism "Insurrectionary anarchism"), and his summaries of Marx's [critique of political economy](/wiki/Critique_of_political_economy "Critique of political economy").[[227]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELéonard2018368-227) Cafiero's summary of *Das Kapital* received numerous editions after his death: a second edition was published in 1913, with a preface by [Luigi Fabbri](/wiki/Luigi_Fabbri "Luigi Fabbri"); a third edition was published in 1920; and following the [fall of the Fascist regime in Italy](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Fascist_regime_in_Italy "Fall of the Fascist regime in Italy"), a fourth edition was published by the Marxist publisher [Samonà e Savelli](/w/index.php?title=Samon%C3%A0_e_Savelli&action=edit&redlink=1 "Samonà e Savelli (page does not exist)") [[it](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Samon%C3%A0_e_Savelli "it:Samonà e Savelli")], which omitted any reference to Cafiero's anarchism. A first edition copy was proudly preserved by [Alessandro Mussolini](/wiki/Alessandro_Mussolini "Alessandro Mussolini"), the father of fascist dictator [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini").[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELéonard2018376–377-203) During the 1960s, while the Italian student Gian Carlo Maffei was browsing the [Swiss Federal Archives](/wiki/Swiss_Federal_Archives "Swiss Federal Archives"), he discovered a manuscript of Cafiero's that had been unpublished since it was seized by police in 1881. From 1970 to 1972, four of Cafiero's essays on the subject of revolution were finally published by Maffei and [Gianni Bosio](/w/index.php?title=Gianni_Bosio&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gianni Bosio (page does not exist)") [[it](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gianni_Bosio "it:Gianni Bosio")].[[218]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDrake200349-218) The final unpublished essay revealed that Cafiero, along with his concerns about capitalism, was worried about the dangers presented by the rise of [authoritarian socialism](/wiki/Authoritarian_socialism "Authoritarian socialism").[[220]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDrake200350-220)

Cafiero was [venerated](/wiki/Veneration "Veneration") by Italian anarchists and socialists of his time,[[228]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPernicone199365-228) and in the wake of his death, he was made into a [martyr](/wiki/Martyr "Martyr") for anarchist communism.[[229]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDrake200354–55-229) Anarchist groups in Italy and America were named after him, while anarchist parents named their children "Cafiero", and artists dedicated several works to him.[[229]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDrake200354–55-229) Cafiero appeared as a character in [Riccardo Bacchelli](/wiki/Riccardo_Bacchelli "Riccardo Bacchelli")'s 1927 novel *[Il Diavolo al Pontelungo](/w/index.php?title=Il_Diavolo_al_Pontelungo&action=edit&redlink=1 "Il Diavolo al Pontelungo (page does not exist)") [[it](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Il_Diavolo_al_Pontelungo "it:Il Diavolo al Pontelungo")]*,[[230]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDrake200354Léonard2018367-230) which was about the Italian anarchist circle around Mikhail Bakunin. While Bacchelli depicted most of the anarchists as "delusional and ineffectual fanatics", Cafiero was portrayed relatively positively as a noble and generous man.[[231]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDrake200354-231) Cafiero was also an inspiration for the main character in [Paolo and Vittorio Taviani](/wiki/Paolo_and_Vittorio_Taviani "Paolo and Vittorio Taviani")'s 1972 film *[St. Michael Had a Rooster](/wiki/St._Michael_Had_a_Rooster "St. Michael Had a Rooster")*, which depicts the tension between [revolutionary](/wiki/Revolutionary_socialism "Revolutionary socialism") and [scientific socialism](/wiki/Scientific_socialism "Scientific socialism") during the insurrections of the 1870s.[[232]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELéonard2018367–368-232)

[Primary sources](/wiki/Primary_source "Primary source") on Cafiero's life have been written by [James Guillaume](/wiki/James_Guillaume "James Guillaume") (who also penned the introduction to the 1910 edition of Cafiero's Summary of Capital) and [Max Nettlau](/wiki/Max_Nettlau "Max Nettlau"), in their respective histories of the IWA.[[233]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELéonard2018366–367-233) Later histories of the [Italian anarchist movement](/wiki/Anarchism_in_Italy "Anarchism in Italy"), written by [Pier Carlo Masini](/w/index.php?title=Pier_Carlo_Masini&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pier Carlo Masini (page does not exist)"), [Enzo Santarelli](/w/index.php?title=Enzo_Santarelli&action=edit&redlink=1 "Enzo Santarelli (page does not exist)"), [T.R. Ravindranathan](/w/index.php?title=T.R._Ravindranathan&action=edit&redlink=1 "T.R. Ravindranathan (page does not exist)") and [Nunzio Pernicone](/w/index.php?title=Nunzio_Pernicone&action=edit&redlink=1 "Nunzio Pernicone (page does not exist)"), situated Cafiero firmly within the anarchist tradition. On the other hand, Marxist historian [Richard Drake](/w/index.php?title=Richard_Drake_(historian)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Richard Drake (historian) (page does not exist)") depicted Cafiero as a "dissident forerunner" of Italian Marxism.[[234]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELéonard2018367-234) His eventual endorsement of electoralism has also been taken by some historians as an example of classical anarchism's defeat by social democracy.[[235]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPernicone1993201–202-235) Overall, Cafiero's ideological trajectory has challenged the dichotomy between Marxism and anarchism as opposing factions.[[236]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELéonard2018377–378-236)
